Project Python for Data Science: Austo Motor Company Analysis¶

Context

Analysts are required to explore data and reflect on the insights. Clear writing skill is an integral part of a good report. Note that the explanations must be such that readers with minimum knowledge of analytics is able to grasp the insight.

Austo Motor Company is a leading car manufacturer specializing in SUV, Sedan, and Hatchback models. In its recent board meeting, concerns were raised by the members on the efficiency of the marketing campaign currently being used. The board decides to rope in an analytics professional to improve the existing campaign.

Objective

They want to analyze the data to get a fair idea about the demand of customers which will help them in enhancing their customer experience. Suppose you are a Data Scientist at the company and the Data Science team has shared some of the key questions that need to be answered. Perform the data analysis to find answers to these questions that will help the company to improve the business.

Data Description

Age: The age of the individual in years.
Gender: The gender of the individual, categorized as male or female.
Profession: The occupation or profession of the individual.
Marital_status: The marital status of the individual, such as married &, single
Education: The educational qualification of the individual Graduate and Post Graduate
No_of_Dependents: The number of dependents (e.g., children, elderly parents) that the individual supports financially.
Personal_loan: A binary variable indicating whether the individual has taken a personal loan "Yes" or "No"
House_loan: A binary variable indicating whether the individual has taken a housing loan "Yes" or "No"
Partner_working: A binary variable indicating whether the individual's partner is employed "Yes" or "No"
Salary: The individual's salary or income.
Partner_salary: The salary or income of the individual's partner, if applicable.
Total_salary: The total combined salary of the individual and their partner (if applicable).
Price: The price of a product or service.
Make: The type of automobile

In [3]:
# importing the libraries to help the reading and manipulating the data set
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

# Libraries to help data visualization of dataset
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

# We are ristricting the float value upto 2 decimal places
pd.set_option('display.float_format', lambda x: '%.2f' % x)

# as female word written in 3 different spelling, replacing the Femal and Femle data with correct spelling Female
df["Gender"] = df["Gender"].replace({"Femal": "Female", "Femle": "Female"})
print(df["Gender"].unique())

Checking Null Value in the dataset¶

pd.DataFrame({'Null Value Count':df.isnull().sum()[df.isnull().sum()>0],'% of Null Value':(df.isnull().sum()[df.isnull().sum()>0]/df.shape[0])*100})

Out[14]:
Gender
Male     0.76
Female   0.21
NaN      0.03
Name: proportion, dtype: float64
  • As we can see that there is missing value 3% in Gender Column
  • The percentage of missing values is very less than the percentage of observations from other Gender.
  • We can treat the missing values with a separate category for this variable as it is very less
In [15]:
# Replacing NaN with Unknown
df['Gender'].fillna('Gender_Unknown', inplace =True)
In [16]:

The missing values in the Gender column have been treated. Let us now move on to Partner_salary variable and see how to deal with the missing values present there.

Since Partner_salary is a numerical variable, so we can impute the missing values by mean or median but before imputation, let's analyze the Partner_salary variable in detail.

Let us print the rows where the Partner_salary variable is having missing values.

*There are 106 observations where Partner_salary variable has missing values. From the overview of the dataset,

  • so we will fill it with median
In [20]:
# Calculate Partner_salary for missing values using Total_salary - Salary
df.loc[df['Partner_salary'].isnull()==True, 'Partner_salary'] = df['Total_salary'] - df['Salary']
In [21]:
# Verify that there are no more missing values in the Partner_salary column
df.isnull().sum()

Statical summary of Numerical Variables¶

In [22]:
df.describe().T
Out[22]:
count mean std min 25% 50% 75% max
Age 1581.00 31.92 8.43 22.00 25.00 29.00 38.00 54.00
No_of_Dependents 1581.00 2.46 0.94 0.00 2.00 2.00 3.00 4.00
Salary 1581.00 60392.22 14674.83 30000.00 51900.00 59500.00 71800.00 99300.00
Partner_salary 1581.00 19233.78 19670.39 0.00 0.00 25100.00 38100.00 80500.00
Total_salary 1581.00 79626.00 25545.86 30000.00 60500.00 78000.00 95900.00 171000.00
Price 1581.00 35597.72 13633.64 18000.00 25000.00 31000.00 47000.00 70000.00

Age: On an average age of an individuals are 31 yrs, 50% of the individual are of 29 yrs. in this dataset. 75% of an individual having age less than or equal to 38 yrs. The minimum and the maximum age of an individual are 22 yrs. and 54 yrs. respectively.

No_of_Dependents: There are 0 no of dependents and few of the dependents is having up to 4 dependents. On an average there are 2 no of dependents of customer are available in dataset. 75% of an individual are having less than or equal to 3 dependents.

Salary: The range of salary from 30,000 to 99,300. 50 % of salary distribution are 59,000. 75% of the salary are less than or equal to 71,800. It is noticeable that there are diverse income levels of the customer.

Partner_salary: The minimum partner salary is showing 0 which indicates many partners may not earn salary. Mean of partner_salary is very less; it shows many partners either does not work or they earn very less salary. On an average partner is earning 25,100. 75% of partner salary is less than or equal to 38,100. The maximum partner salary is 80,500.

Total_salary: It reflects the total household earning i.e. salary + partner_salary. On an average the total salary is 79,262. The range of total salary are 30,000 to 171000. 50% of the total salary lies at 78,000. There is a wide range of salary due to salary of an individuals and partner salary.

Price: on an average the price of car is 35597. Median of the price of car is 31,000

Outlier Detection and Treatment¶

In [37]:
# outlier detection using boxplot
# selecting the numerical columns of data and adding their names in a list
numeric_columns = ['Age','No_of_Dependents','Salary','Partner_salary','Total_salary', 'Price']
plt.figure(figsize=(15, 12))

for i, variable in enumerate(numeric_columns):
    plt.subplot(3, 3, i + 1)
    plt.boxplot(df[variable], whis=1.5)
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.title(variable)

plt.show()
In [38]:
num_columns = ['Age','No_of_Dependents','Salary','Partner_salary','Total_salary', 'Price']
Q1 = df[num_columns].quantile(0.25)
Q3 = df[num_columns].quantile(0.75)

IQR = Q3 - Q1
lower_whisker = Q1 - 1.5*IQR
upper_whisker = Q3 + 1.5*IQR
In [39]:
# Percentage of outliers in each column
((df[num_columns] < lower_whisker) | (df[num_columns] > upper_whisker)).sum()
Out[39]:
Age                  0
No_of_Dependents    20
Salary               0
Partner_salary       0
Total_salary        27
Price                0
dtype: int64
In [40]:
def treat_outliers(df, col):

    Q1 = df[col].quantile(0.25)  # 25th quantile
    Q3 = df[col].quantile(0.75)  # 75th quantile
    IQR = Q3 - Q1                # Inter Quantile Range (75th perentile - 25th percentile)

    # all the values smaller than lower_whisker will be assigned the value of lower_whisker
    lower_whisker = Q1 - 1.5 * IQR
    # all the values greater than upper_whisker will be assigned the value of upper_whisker
    upper_whisker = Q3 + 1.5 * IQR

    # the assignment will be done by using the clip function of NumPy
    df[col] = np.clip(df[col], lower_whisker, upper_whisker)

    return df
In [41]:
plt.figure(figsize=(7,2))
data = treat_outliers(df,'No_of_Dependents')
# visualizing the column after outlier treatment
sns.boxplot(data=df,x='No_of_Dependents', color = "g")
plt.show()
In [42]:
plt.figure(figsize=(7,2))
data = treat_outliers(df,'Total_salary')
# visualizing the column after outlier treatment
sns.boxplot(data=df,x='Total_salary', color = "r")
plt.show()

  1. Do men tend to prefer SUVs more compared to women?

The percentage of individuals preferring SUVs by gender is: The data shows that 9.84% of men prefer SUVs compared to 52.29% of women (note: there are some data entries with typos in gender).

KEY Question¶

In [ ]:
df["Profession"].unique()
Out[ ]:
array(['Business', 'Salaried'], dtype=object)
In [ ]:
# Preference of make type by gender
suv_prefer = df[df["Make"]=="SUV"].groupby("Gender").size().reset_index(name = "count")
total_counts = df.groupby("Gender").size().reset_index(name = "total_count")
prefer = pd.merge(suv_prefer, total_counts, on = "Gender", how = "outer")
prefer["SUV_Prefer_percentage"] = (prefer["count"]/prefer["total_count"])*100
prefer
Out[ ]:
Gender count total_count SUV_Prefer_percentage
0 Female 173 329 52.58
1 Gender_Unknown 6 53 11.32
2 Male 118 1199 9.84
In [ ]:
plt.figure(figsize=(7, 4))
sns.barplot(data=prefer, x="Gender", y="SUV_Prefer_percentage", hue = "Gender") # Changed x to "Gender" as "Make" does not exist in the DataFrame

# Customize the plot
plt.title("Preference of SUV by Gender")
plt.ylabel("SUV Preference Percentage")
plt.xlabel("Gender")

# Display the plot
plt.show()
  1. What is the likelihood of a salaried person buying a Sedan?
In [ ]:
# Calculate the likelihood of a salaried person buying a Sedan
salaried_sedan = df[(df['Profession'] == 'Salaried') & (df['Make'] == 'Sedan')].shape[0]
total_salaried = df[df['Profession'] == 'Salaried'].shape[0]
likelihood = (salaried_sedan / total_salaried) * 100

likelihood
Out[ ]:
44.19642857142857

The likelihood of a salaried person buying a Sedan is approximately 44.20%.

  1. Evidence for Sheldon Cooper's claim that a salaried male is an easier target for an SUV sale over a Sedan sale.
In [ ]:
salaried_male_suv = df[(df['Profession'] == 'Salaried') & (df['Gender'] == 'Male') & (df['Make'] == 'SUV')].shape[0]
salaried_male_sedan = df[(df['Profession'] == 'Salaried') & (df['Gender'] == 'Male') & (df['Make'] == 'Sedan')].shape[0]
total_salaried_male = df[(df['Profession'] == 'Salaried') & (df['Gender'] == 'Male')].shape[0]

# Calculating the percentage of salaried male and sedan male
percentage_salaried_male_suv = (salaried_male_suv / total_salaried_male) * 100
percentage_salaried_male_sedan = (salaried_male_sedan / total_salaried_male) * 100
print("Salaried male preferring SUV :", salaried_male_suv)
print("Percentage of Salaried male preferring SUV :",percentage_salaried_male_suv)
print("Salaried male preferring Seadan :",salaried_male_sedan)
print("Percentage of Salaried male preferring Seadan :",percentage_salaried_male_sedan)

Salaried male preferring SUV : 85
Percentage of Salaried male preferring SUV : 13.28125
Salaried male preferring Seadan : 279
Percentage of Salaried male preferring Seadan : 43.59375

  • Filter the data for salaried males and count the preference for SUVs and sedans

  • Salaried male preferring SUV : 85

  • Percentage of Salaried male preferring SUV : 13.28125

  • Salaried male preferring Seadan : 279

  • Percentage of Salaried male preferring Seadan : 43.59375

In [ ]:
# Visualization with graph
labels = ['SUV', 'Sedan']
counts = [salaried_male_suv, salaried_male_sedan]
percentages = [percentage_salaried_male_suv, percentage_salaried_male_sedan]

fig, ax1 = plt.subplots()

# Plotting the count bar chart
ax1.bar(labels, counts, color=['blue', 'orange'])
ax1.set_ylabel('Counts', color='black')
ax1.set_title('Preferences of Salaried Males for SUVs and Sedans')

# Creating a second y-axis to plot the percentages
ax2 = ax1.twinx()
ax2.plot(labels, percentages, color='green', marker='o')
ax2.set_ylabel('Percentage (%)', color='green')
ax2.set_xlabel('Car Make', color='green')

# Displaying the plot
plt.show()

4. How does the amount spent on purchasing automobiles vary by gender?

In [ ]:
# Group by gender and calculate the average and total amount spent on automobiles
amount_spent_by_gender = df.groupby('Gender')['Price'].agg(['mean', 'sum']).reset_index()
amount_spent_by_gender
Out[ ]:
Gender mean sum
0 Female 47705.17 15695000
1 Gender_Unknown 23339.62 1237000
2 Male 32817.35 39348000
In [ ]:
gender = ['Female', 'Gender_Unknown', 'Male']
total_spent = [47705.17, 23339.62, 32817.35]
# Create the pie chart
plt.pie(total_spent, labels=gender, autopct='%1.1f%%')
# Set the title
_ = plt.title('Total Spending by Gender')

Observation:

  • On average, females spend more on automobiles than males.

5. How much money was spent on purchasing automobiles by individuals who took a personal loan?

In [ ]:
# Filter the data for individuals who took a personal loan and calculate the total amount spent
total_amount_personal_loan = df[df['Personal_loan'] == 'Yes']['Price'].sum()
total_amount_personal_loan
Out[ ]:
27290000

Total amount spent by individuals with personal loans is 27,290,000.

6. How does having a working partner influence the purchase of higher-priced cars?

Percentage of higher-priced car purchases: With a working partner: 37.56% Without a working partner: 38.29% Having a working partner does not significantly influence the purchase of higher-priced cars compared to not having a working partner.

In [ ]:
# Define higher-priced cars
high_priced = df['Price'].median()

# Filter data to get high-priced purchases
priced_purchases = df[df['Price'] > high_priced]

# Group by working partner status and calculate the mean amount spent
influence = priced_purchases.groupby('Partner_working')['Price'].mean().reset_index()

influence
Out[ ]:
Partner_working Price
0 No 47064.61
1 Yes 46335.70
In [ ]:
plt.figure(figsize=(7, 4))
ax = sns.barplot(data=influence, x="Partner_working", y="Price", color = "r")
plt.xlabel('Partner Working')
plt.ylabel('Average Price')
plt.title('Average Price by Partner Working Status')
for bars in ax.containers:
    ax.bar_label(bars)
plt.show()

Observation:

  • Individuals without a working partner tend to spend slightly more on higher-priced cars than those with a working partner.

The analysis shows that while more women (percentage-wise) prefer SUVs, salaried males are more inclined towards Sedans. Salaried individuals have a significant likelihood of purchasing Sedans. The data also indicates that having a working partner has a minimal impact on the likelihood of purchasing higher-priced cars. The amount spent on automobiles varies by gender, with females on average spending more than males. ​
